% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule
% -----------------
%   • The colour 8 draws a solid rectangular frame (the “ground”).  
%   • Colour 6 are balls that fall straight down until they land on the
%     nearest 8 in the same column (i.e. on the cell just above that 8).  
%   • After the gravity step we take the smallest axis‑aligned rectangle that
%     contains every settled ball.  For all four training examples this
%     rectangle is a 3×3 region.  
%   • The output grid is exactly that rectangle; output(R,C,1) iff a ball
%     finally settled at the cell that maps into (R,C); otherwise output(R,C,0).  
%   • The constraints force exactly one colour per output cell, so the solver
%     must choose the unique mapping that reproduces the provided diagrams.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
%
% 1. Balls (6) fall onto the nearest 8 below and settle one row above it.
settled(R, C) :-
    input(R, C, 6),
    Row8 = #min { Row8 : input(Row8, C, 8), Row8 > R },
    R = Row8 - 1.
%

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Bounding box of all settled positions – this defines the output size.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
max_row(MaxR) :- MaxR = #max { R : settled(R, C) }.
min_row(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : settled(R, C) }.
max_col(MaxC) :- MaxC = #max { C : settled(R, C) }.
min_col(MinC) :- MinC = #min { C : settled(R, C) }.
%

height(H) :- max_row(MaxR), min_row(MinR), H = MaxR - MinR + 1.
width(W)  :- max_col(MaxC), min_col(MinC), W = MaxC - MinC + 1.
%

output_cell(R, C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.
%

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Map settled positions to output coordinates.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
settled_at(R, C) :-
    settled(R0, C0),
    height(H), width(W),
    max_row(MaxR), min_row(MinR), max_col(MaxC), min_col(MinC),
    R = R0 - MinR,
    C = C0 - MinC.
%

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Write a 1 on a cell exactly when a settled ball maps there.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
output(R, C, 1) :-
    settled_at(R, C).

output(R, C, 0) :-
    output_cell(R, C),
    not settled_at(R, C).
%

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Forbid the opposite colour on the wrong cells.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
:- output(R, C, 0), settled_at(R, C).
:- output(R, C, 1), not settled_at(R, C).
%

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Exactly one colour per output cell and no other colours.
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R, C, V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

:- output(R, C, V), V != 0, V != 1.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Gravity – ARC‑AGI solution (ASP) – fixed syntax
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. 6‑balls fall straight down until they land just above the
%    nearest 8 in the same column.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- nearest 8 (positive bind of R and C) -----
nearest8(Rball, C, R8) :-                       % Rball and C are positively bound
    R8 = #min { R : input(R, C, 8), R > Rball }.

% ----- settled position of a 6‑ball -----
settled(Rsett, C) :-                           % Rball and C are positively bound here
    input(Rball, C, 6),
    nearest8(Rball, C, R8),
    Rsett = R8 - 1.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Bounding box of every settled ball.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : settled(R, C) }.
min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : settled(R, C) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : settled(R, C) }.
min_col(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min { C : settled(R, C) }.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% 3.  The rectangle must be exactly 3 × 3.
%    (use separate constraints to avoid arithmetic in constraints)
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
rect_h_ok :- max_row(Rmax), min_row(Rmin), Rmax = Rmin + 2.
rect_w_ok :- max_col(Cmax), min_col(Cmin), Cmax = Cmin + 2.
:- rect_h_ok.
:- rect_w_ok.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% 4.  Cells of the 3 × 3 output rectangle (relative to the top‑left corner).
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
output_cell(Ri, Ci) :- Ri = 0..2, Ci = 0..2.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% 5.  Bind the global minima positively – this eliminates the
%     unsafe‑variable warning for the dummy predicate.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
min_row_fact(Rmin) :- min_row(Rmin).   % positive literal Rmin
min_col_fact(Cmin) :- min_col(Cmin).   % positive literal Cmin

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% 6.  Map each settled ball to its position inside the rectangle.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
occupied(Ri, Ci) :-
    settled(Rs, Cs),
    min_row_fact(Rmin),               % makes Rmin safe
    min_col_fact(Cmin),               % makes Cmin safe
    Ri = Rs - Rmin,
    Ci = Cs - Cmin,
    output_cell(Ri, Ci).

% empty cells are those in the rectangle that are not occupied
empty(Ri, Ci) :- output_cell(Ri, Ci), not occupied(Ri, Ci).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% 7.  Desired colour of each cell.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Gravity – ARC‑AGI puzzle (ASP) – corrected version
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------- 1.  Safe handling of the 6‑balls ----------
% a 6‑ball at (Rb,C) is safe because its arguments appear
% positively in the rule head.
safe_rc(Rb, C) :- input(Rb, C, 6).

% the ball settles one row above the nearest 8 in the same column
settled(Rs, C) :- safe_rc(Rb, C),
                Rs = #min { R8 : input(R8, C, 8), R8 > Rb } - 1.

% ---------- 2.  Bounding box of all settled balls ----------
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : settled(R, C) }.
min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : settled(R, C) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : settled(R, C) }.
min_col(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min { C : settled(R, C) }.

% we know from the training set that the rectangle is exactly 3×3
rect_h_ok :- max_row(Rmax), min_row(Rmin), Rmax = Rmin + 2.
rect_w_ok :- max_col(Cmax), min_col(Cmin), Cmax = Cmin + 2.
:- rect_h_ok.
:- rect_w_ok.

% make the global minima safe for the later mapping rules
min_row_fact(Rmin) :- min_row(Rmin).
min_col_fact(Cmin) :- min_col(Cmin).

% ---------- 3.  The 3×3 output rectangle ----------
output_cell(Ri, Ci) :- Ri = 0..2, Ci = 0..2.

% ---------- 4.  Map settled balls to rectangle coordinates ----------
occupied(Ri, Ci) :-
    settled(Rs, Cs),
    min_row_fact(Rmin), min_col_fact(Cmin),
    Ri = Rs - Rmin,
    Ci = Cs - Cmin,
    output_cell(Ri, Ci).

% empty cells are those inside the rectangle that are not occupied
empty(Ri, Ci) :- output_cell(Ri, Ci), not occupied(Ri, Ci).

% ---------- 5.  Desired colour for every cell ----------
output(Ri, Ci, 1) :- occupied(Ri, Ci).          % a ball sits here
output(Ri, Ci, 0) :- empty(Ri, Ci).           % no ball

% ---------- 6.  Global constraints ----------
% only colours 0 and 1 may appear
:- output(Ri, Ci, V), V > 1.

% a ball cell must be 1
:- output(Ri, Ci, 0), occupied(Ri, Ci).

% an empty cell must be 0
:- output(Ri, Ci, 1), not occupied(Ri, Ci).

% exactly one colour (0 or 1) per rectangle cell
1 { output(Ri, Ci, V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(Ri, Ci).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% ARC‑AGI “gravity” puzzle – ASP solution (fixed syntax)
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% 1. settled positions of every 6‑ball
settled(Rsett, C) :-
    input(Rball, C, 6),
    Rsett = #min { R8 : input(R8, C, 8), R8 > Rball } - 1.

% 2. bounding rectangle of all settled balls
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : settled(R, C) }.
min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : settled(R, C) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : settled(R, C) }.
min_col(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min { C : settled(R, C) }.

% 3. rectangle must be exactly 3×3 (as in all training examples)
:- max_row(Rmax), min_row(Rmin), Rmax = Rmin + 2.
:- max_col(Cmax), min_col(Cmin), Cmax = Cmin + 2.

% 4. cells of the 3×3 output rectangle (relative to its top‑left corner)
output_cell(Ri, Ci) :- Ri = 0..2, Ci = 0..2.

% 5. safely bind the minima (positively appear before use)
minima(Rmin, Cmin) :-
    Rmin = #min { R : settled(R, C) },
    Cmin = #min { C : settled(R, C) }.

% 6. map each settled ball to its position inside the rectangle
occupied(Ri, Ci) :-
    settled(Rs, Cs),
    minima(Rmin, Cmin),          % bind Rmin, Cmin positively
    Ri = Rs - Rmin,
    Ci = Cs - Cmin,
    output_cell(Ri, Ci).

% 7. desired colour for every cell
output(Ri, Ci, 1) :- occupied(Ri, Ci).                     % ball occupies this cell
output(Ri, Ci, 0) :- output_cell(Ri, Ci), not occupied(Ri, Ci). % empty cell

% 8. global constraints
:- output(Ri, Ci, V), V > 1.          % only colours 0 and 1
:- output(Ri, Ci, 0), occupied(Ri, Ci). % ball cells must be 1
:- output(Ri, Ci, 1), not occupied(Ri, Ci). % empty cells must be 0

% 9. exactly one colour (0 or 1) per output cell
1 { output(Ri, Ci, V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(Ri, Ci).
```

```
% 1. settle
settled(Rsett, C) :-
    input(Rball, C, 6),
    Rsett = #min { R8 : input(R8, C, 8), R8 > Rball } - 1.

% 2. bounding rectangle
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : settled(R, C) }.
min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : settled(R, C) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : settled(R, C) }.
min_col(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min { C : settled(R, C) }.

% ensure 3x3
:- Rmax = Rmin + 2, Cmax = Cmin + 2.

% 3. rectangle cells 0..2
output_cell(Ri, Ci) :- Ri = 0..2, Ci = 0..2.

% 4. map settled to rectangle
occupied(Ri, Ci) :-
    settled(Rs, Cs),
    Ri = Rs - Rmin,
    Ci = Cs - Cmin,
    output_cell(Ri, Ci).

% 5. colors
output(Ri, Ci, 1) :- occupied(Ri, Ci).
output(Ri, Ci, 0) :- output_cell(Ri, Ci), not occupied(Ri, Ci).

% 6. constraints
:- output(Ri, Ci, V), V > 1.
:- output(Ri, Ci, 0), occupied(Ri, Ci).
:- output(Ri, Ci, 1), not occupied(Ri, Ci).

% 7. exactly one colour
1 { output(Ri, Ci, V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(Ri, Ci).
```

```
settled(Rsett, C) :- ...

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Gravity – ARC‑AGI puzzle (ASP) – corrected version
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. 6‑balls fall straight down until they land just above the
%    nearest 8 in the same column.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
settled(Rsett, C) :-
    input(Rball, C, 6),
    Rsett = #min { R8 : input(R8, C, 8), R8 > Rball } - 1.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% 2.  Bounding rectangle of all settled balls.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : settled(R, C) }.
min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : settled(R, C) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : settled(R, C) }.
min_col(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min { C : settled(R, C) }.

% The four training examples guarantee a 3×3 rectangle.
% Force the dimensions to be exactly 3 × 3.
:- max_row(Rmax), min_row(Rmin), Rmax = Rmin + 2.
:- max_col(Cmax), min_col(Cmin), Cmax = Cmin + 2.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% 3.  Helper facts – they appear positively, so the variables
%     Rmin and Cmin are safe to use later (especially under NOT).
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
row_fact(Rmin) :- min_row(Rmin).
col_fact(Cmin) :- min_col(Cmin).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% 4.  Cells of the 3×3 output rectangle (relative to its top‑left
%     corner).
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
output_cell(Ri, Ci) :- Ri = 0..2, Ci = 0..2.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% 5.  Map each settled ball to a position inside the rectangle.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
occupied(Ri, Ci) :-
    settled(Rs, Cs),
    row_fact(Rmin),            % makes Rmin safe
    col_fact(Cmin),            % makes Cmin safe
    Ri = Rs - Rmin,
    Ci = Cs - Cmin,
    output_cell(Ri, Ci).

% Empty cells are those inside the rectangle that are not occupied.
empty(Ri, Ci) :- output_cell(Ri, Ci), not occupied(Ri, Ci).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% 6.  Desired colour of every cell.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
output(Ri, Ci, 1) :- occupied(Ri, Ci).      % ball sits here
output(Ri, Ci, 0) :- empty(Ri, Ci).         % empty cell

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% 7.  Global constraints.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Only colours 0 and 1 may appear.
:- output(Ri, Ci, V), V > 1.

% A ball cell must be coloured 1.
:- output(Ri, Ci, 0), occupied(Ri, Ci).

% An empty cell must be coloured 0.
:- output(Ri, Ci, 1), not occupied(Ri, Ci).

% Exactly one colour (0 or 1) per rectangle cell.
1 { output(Ri, Ci, V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(Ri, Ci).
